---
title: "Long COVID, Post-Exertional Malaise, Pacing and Recovery"
subtitle: "A Research Synthesis"
date: 2026-03-02
bibliography: ../references.bib
csl: https://raw.githubusercontent.com/citation-style-language/styles/master/apa.csl
format:
  html:
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 3
    number-sections: true
---

A research synthesis on post-COVID syndrome (Long COVID), post-exertional malaise (PEM), rehabilitation approaches, pacing strategies, and recovery trajectories.

## What Is Long COVID?

Long COVID (also called post-COVID condition or post-acute sequelae of SARS-CoV-2 / PASC) refers to persistent symptoms lasting weeks to years after acute SARS-CoV-2 infection. The WHO defines it as symptoms present at least 3 months after probable or confirmed infection, lasting at least 2 months, and not explained by an alternative diagnosis.

Prevalence estimates vary widely. A systematic review and meta-analysis found that roughly 45% of COVID survivors experience at least one unresolved symptom at 4 months, declining to approximately 30% by 12 months, though some studies report persistent symptoms in 10--25% of non-hospitalised patients [@davis2023longcovid]. The most commonly reported symptoms include fatigue (57%), post-exertional malaise (46%), cognitive dysfunction or "brain fog" (31%), dyspnoea, pain, and sleep disturbance [@alaly2024policy; @rahmati2025sequelae].

Long COVID is not a single disease but rather a collection of overlapping syndromes, with distinct phenotypic clusters identified in longitudinal cohort data. The NIH RECOVER-Adult cohort identified subgroups including those with persistently high symptom burden (~5%), intermittently high burden (~12%), and a late-onset group (~14%) whose symptoms increased rather than resolved over 15 months [@recover2025trajectories].

## Pathophysiology and Mechanisms

Research has identified several interconnected mechanisms that drive Long COVID [@peluso2024mechanisms; @bohmwald2024pathophysiology; @thierry2025microclots].

![Conceptual model for Long COVID mechanisms: upstream biological disruptions (viral persistence, immune dysregulation) lead to downstream pathophysiology (organ damage, nervous system disturbances, mitochondrial dysfunction). Reproduced from @peluso2024mechanisms.](https://cdn.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/blobs/29ff/11455603/258706b4e462/nihms-2025755-f0003.jpg){#fig-mechanisms}

### Viral Persistence

SARS-CoV-2 RNA and protein have been detected in tissue reservoirs (gut, brain, lungs, lymph nodes) months to years post-infection. Persistent spike protein RNA has been identified in gut tissue for over 2 years in some Long COVID patients. This viral persistence may continually stimulate immune responses and tissue damage [@peluso2024mechanisms; @bohmwald2024pathophysiology].

### Immune Dysregulation

Patients display altered immune profiles, including T-cell exhaustion, persistent activation of inflammatory pathways, cytokine imbalances, and autoantibody production. The immune system appears chronically activated yet functionally impaired [@peluso2024mechanisms; @bohmwald2024pathophysiology; @thierry2025microclots].

### Microclots and Endothelial Dysfunction

Elevated levels of fibrin(ogen) amyloid microclots and abnormal clotting have been found in Long COVID patients. The spike protein can bind fibrinogen, producing structurally abnormal clots resistant to normal breakdown by plasmin. These microclots may obstruct microcirculation, contributing to tissue hypoxia and organ dysfunction [@thierry2025microclots].

### Autonomic Dysfunction

Dysautonomia is prevalent in Long COVID. A meta-analysis found pooled prevalence estimates of 71% for orthostatic intolerance, 36% for postural orthostatic tachycardia syndrome (POTS), and 19% for orthostatic hypotension among post-COVID patients [@wang2026orthostaticprevalence]. In follow-up cohorts, 72% of Long COVID patients had moderate-to-severe autonomic dysfunction at a median of 36 months post-infection [@autonomicburden2025].

### Mitochondrial and Skeletal Muscle Dysfunction

Long COVID is associated with intrinsic skeletal muscle mitochondrial dysfunction, reduced oxidative phosphorylation capacity, a shift towards more glycolytic (fast-twitch) muscle fibres, and endothelial abnormalities --- all of which contribute to exercise intolerance [@appelman2024muscle; @charlton2025skeletal]. Muscle biopsies in Long COVID patients reveal lower succinate dehydrogenase activity, amyloid-containing deposits, and immune cell infiltration that worsens after exertion [@appelman2024muscle].

## Post-Exertional Malaise (PEM)

Post-exertional malaise --- also called post-exertional symptom exacerbation (PESE) --- is the hallmark symptom that distinguishes Long COVID and ME/CFS from other fatigue-related conditions. It is defined as a disproportionate worsening of symptoms following physical, cognitive, or emotional exertion, typically delayed by 12--72 hours and lasting days to weeks [@vernon2023pem].

### Prevalence and Impact

A systematic review with meta-analysis found that approximately 25% of community-dwelling adults with post-COVID condition experience PEM [@pouliopoulou2025pem]. PEM is associated with more severe disease phenotypes, worse functional outcomes, and greater difficulty returning to work or daily activities.

### Physiological Basis

PEM has an established physiological basis. @appelman2024muscle showed that exercise-induced myopathy, metabolic disturbances, and tissue infiltration of amyloid-containing deposits in skeletal muscle all worsen after PEM is triggered. @charlton2025skeletal identified intrinsic mitochondrial dysfunction, endothelial abnormalities, and a muscle fibre type shift towards a more glycolytic phenotype as main contributors to exercise intolerance, noting that rapid skeletal muscle tissue damage and intramuscular immune cell infiltration contribute to PEM symptoms.

![Skeletal muscle pathology in Long COVID. Exercise triggers worsening of muscle abnormalities including mitochondrial dysfunction, immune infiltration, and amyloid deposits. Reproduced from @appelman2024muscle.](https://media.springernature.com/full/springer-static/image/art%3A10.1038%2Fs41467-023-44432-3/MediaObjects/41467_2023_44432_Fig1_HTML.png){#fig-muscle}

### Two-Day CPET as Diagnostic Tool

Two-day cardiopulmonary exercise testing (CPET) --- performing maximal exercise tests 24 hours apart --- has emerged as an objective tool for characterising PEM. Submaximal two-day CPET studies found that VO~2~ decreased from Day 1 to Day 2 and work rate at ventilatory threshold was lower on Day 2, objectively demonstrating impaired recovery capacity [@stussman2025pem]. However, one study using maximal two-day CPET in 15 Long COVID patients found no significant differences between days, highlighting the heterogeneity of PEM presentation and the importance of test protocol design [@gattoni2025cpet].

### Long COVID PEM vs. ME/CFS PEM

PEM in Long COVID closely resembles PEM in myalgic encephalomyelitis/chronic fatigue syndrome (ME/CFS). @vernon2023pem compared PEM characteristics and found substantial overlap, with both groups reporting similar severity, trigger patterns, and recovery timelines. Acknowledging PEM as the cardinal symptom in specialist healthcare settings improves patient satisfaction and reduces clinical deterioration [@strand2023focus].

## Why Graded Exercise Therapy (GET) Is Contraindicated

### NICE 2021 Guidelines

The UK National Institute for Health and Care Excellence (NICE) updated its ME/CFS guidelines in 2021, making a landmark recommendation: graded exercise therapy should not be offered. The guideline concluded that any programme based on fixed incremental increases in physical activity is potentially harmful to people with ME/CFS and, by extension, to Long COVID patients with PEM [@vink2022nice].

### Evidence of Harm

Patient surveys consistently show that between 50% and 74% of ME/CFS patients report that GET worsened their symptoms. The PACE trial, long cited as supporting GET, was found to have substantial protocol deviations and retrospective adjustment of recovery criteria; a reanalysis showed that GET was ineffective [@vink2022nice]. The American Heart Association's 2025 scientific statement acknowledged that conventional exercise prescription approaches may not apply to Long COVID patients with exercise intolerance and PEM [@cornwell2025aha].

### The Physiological Problem with GET

People with PEM tend to have much lower than expected VO~2~ max. GET is designed around the principle that deconditioning can be reversed through progressive overload, but PEM represents a fundamentally different problem: exertion triggers pathological responses in muscle tissue, immune function, and metabolism rather than adaptive training responses [@appelman2024muscle; @charlton2025skeletal; @cornwell2025aha].

## Pacing as a Management Strategy

### The Energy Envelope Theory

Pacing is rooted in the Energy Envelope Theory (EET), originally developed for ME/CFS. The theory posits that individuals have a limited "energy envelope" --- a finite amount of available energy --- and that staying within this envelope prevents PEM episodes and may allow gradual improvement [@jason2013envelope; @oconnor2019envelope]. Research using cluster analysis confirmed that ME/CFS patients who stayed within their energy envelope reported fewer symptoms, better quality of life, and were more likely to improve physical functioning and reduce fatigue severity [@brown2013envelope].

### Definition and Principles

Pacing (also called energy management or activity management) involves:

- **Identifying the energy envelope**: understanding personal limits for physical, cognitive, and emotional activity
- **Activity-rest cycling**: alternating periods of activity with planned rest, rather than pushing through symptoms
- **Pre-emptive rest**: resting before reaching the point of symptom exacerbation
- **Symptom-contingent activity**: adjusting daily activity levels based on current symptom severity rather than following a fixed schedule
- **Avoiding boom-bust cycles**: preventing the pattern of overexertion on "good days" followed by prolonged crashes

### Evidence for Pacing in Long COVID

A scoping review of pacing for ME/CFS drew lessons for the Long COVID pandemic, concluding that pacing is the most commonly used and most positively rated self-management strategy among patients [@sanalhayes2023pacing].

The PACELOC trial --- a prospective cohort study using an 8-week WHO Borg CR-10 pacing protocol --- demonstrated that a personalised pacing and active rest rehabilitation programme significantly reduced post-exertional symptom exacerbation and improved health status in Long COVID patients [@godfrey2025paceloc].

The PaceMe app study found that an adaptive pacing intervention helped Long COVID patients through four key mechanisms: improved PEM management, a sense of support, validation of their experience, and restored control and agency over their daily lives [@meach2024paceme].

The systematic review by @pouliopoulou2025pem found that five studies showed a decrease in the frequency and intensity of PEM episodes after tailored rehabilitation programmes centred on pacing.

### Heart Rate Monitor-Based Pacing

A feasibility study examined pacing with a heart rate monitor for people with ME/CFS and Long COVID. The approach aims to keep heart rates below the anaerobic threshold --- the point where energy production shifts from aerobic to anaerobic metabolism. Participants reported that heart rate monitoring helped them better understand and avoid PEM by providing an objective signal to rest before exceeding their energy envelope [@mcburney2025hrm].

## Rehabilitation Approaches

### The Case for Symptom-Titrated Exercise

For Long COVID patients *without* PEM, conventional exercise rehabilitation has shown beneficial effects. A meta-analysis of 23 studies (n=1,579) found that exercise-based rehabilitation improved dyspnoea, fatigue, depression, and quality of life [@zheng2024exercise]. However, these studies largely did not screen for or manage PEM --- a critical limitation.

### Stratified Exercise Recommendations

@gloeckl2024recommendations published best-practice recommendations for exercise training in Long COVID, stratified by PEM status:

- **No PEM**: standard exercise rehabilitation principles may be applied, with careful monitoring
- **Mild PEM**: symptom-titrated, low-intensity exercise with strict rest periods; activity should remain well below symptom thresholds
- **Moderate to severe PEM**: pacing and energy management should be the primary intervention; any physical activity must remain within the energy envelope; rest is a therapeutic intervention, not a failure of motivation

![Exercise training recommendations stratified by PEM severity. Reproduced from @gloeckl2024recommendations.](https://media.springernature.com/full/springer-static/image/art%3A10.1186%2Fs40798-024-00695-8/MediaObjects/40798_2024_695_Fig1_HTML.png){#fig-stratified}

### Multidisciplinary Rehabilitation

The BMJ living systematic review [@zeraatkar2024interventions] analysed 24 trials (n=3,695) and found moderate-certainty evidence that combined physical and mental health rehabilitation programmes probably improve overall health outcomes for Long COVID patients. Rehabilitation strategies integrating human factors engineering principles --- including energy management, cognitive support, and symptom-contingent activity --- show particular promise [@rehab2026humanfactors].

### Cognitive Rehabilitation

Brain fog remains one of the most disabling Long COVID symptoms. The NIH RECOVER-NEURO trial tested three non-drug cognitive interventions; while early results were mixed, constraint-induced cognitive therapy (CICT) has shown promise for reducing brain fog and improving instrumental activities of daily living [@palau2025exercise].

## Recovery Trajectories

Recovery from Long COVID is variable and often non-linear. Key findings from longitudinal studies:

- A four-year prospective cohort study found that fatigue (57%), PEM (46%), cough (41%), and cognitive dysfunction (31%) were the most persistent symptoms. Female sex and diabetes independently predicted Long COVID [@alaly2024policy].
- The RECOVER-Adult cohort identified distinct trajectories: some patients recover within months, others plateau, and a subset develops increasing symptoms over time. Approximately 5% showed persistently high symptom burden over 15 months [@recover2025trajectories].
- The Lancet Regional Health CO-FLOW study showed PEM was common in hospitalised patients up to 3 years post-discharge and was linked to a more severe phenotype of Long COVID [@coflow2025].

Importantly, recovery does not always mean cure. Many patients learn to manage their condition through pacing, achieve functional improvement within their energy envelope, and gradually expand their capacity over months to years --- but this requires respecting physiological limits rather than pushing past them [@sanalhayes2023pacing; @godfrey2025paceloc; @meach2024paceme].

## Summary of Key Takeaways

1. **Long COVID is a multi-system disease** with established pathophysiological mechanisms including viral persistence, immune dysregulation, microclots, autonomic dysfunction, and mitochondrial impairment.

2. **PEM is the defining feature** that makes Long COVID rehabilitation fundamentally different from typical post-illness reconditioning. It has a physiological basis in skeletal muscle and metabolic dysfunction.

3. **Graded exercise therapy is contraindicated** for patients with PEM. The NICE 2021 guidelines removed GET from ME/CFS treatment, and evidence shows it can cause harm in patients whose exercise intolerance stems from pathological rather than deconditioning-related mechanisms.

4. **Pacing is the evidence-based approach** for managing PEM. It involves staying within the energy envelope, using activity-rest cycling, and adjusting activity based on symptoms rather than following fixed incremental schedules.

5. **Rehabilitation must be stratified** by PEM status. Patients without PEM may benefit from conventional exercise programmes. Patients with PEM need pacing-first approaches where rest is viewed as therapeutic.

6. **Recovery is possible but variable**. It often requires months to years, is non-linear, and depends on respecting physiological limits rather than "pushing through."

## References {.unnumbered}

::: {#refs}
:::
